narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 stood out as the strongest positive factor in the model's assessment of your law school application, as it suggested a solid foundation that nudged the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar exam on your first try—though it was still a bit below the 3.24 average among students who succeeded. In contrast, your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades worked against you, pushing the model toward predicting a higher risk of failure because it indicated performance lagging behind the typical fifth or sixth decile rank seen in passers. Your LSAT score of 25.50 also contributed to that concern, as it fell well short of the 37.27 average for those who passed, reinforcing the model's worry about bar exam readiness. Overall, the model estimated a 43.2% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt, which crossed the threshold for denying admission since the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and avoid the challenges of retakes. While your middle family income bracket aligns with over a third of successful students and your plan to attend full-time matches nearly all of them, these didn't outweigh the key academic signals. Compared to admitted students, your profile showed promise in undergraduate work but trailed in later school rankings and standardized testing, areas where the model places heavy weight. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors here—but a data-driven call based on patterns from past bar takers. The denial stings, especially with your full-time commitment and steady family support, yet it highlights where gaps appeared relative to proven paths to passing. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the successful average, while strengthening your upper-year performance through targeted study or tutoring. You might also explore programs with bar passage support or gap-year experiences to build a stronger case. Many students in similar spots have rebounded by addressing these exact areas—your undergraduate GPA shows you have the capability. Keep pushing; reapplying with improvements could shift the odds significantly in your favor.
